title: The reservoir that refills itself
subtitle: A five-minute visualisation for the fear that money runs out.
excerpt: A five-minute guided visualisation for the fear of running out. A single image (a stone reservoir, water leaving and water arriving at the same pace) held in mind for the practice. Use when the running-out loop is loud or the day has been spent watching the balance.
sourceNotes: Reservoir / water-as-money imagery is a public-domain visualisation lineage, appearing across modern money-mindset work and in older folk-prosperity traditions. The specific five-minute single-image form is drawn from Day 2 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Release fear of running out".

practice.whenToUse: Use when the running-out loop has arrived — the day spent watching the balance, the bills feeling like a leak, the sense that the next inflow has to fill an empty bucket.

content.text: A five-minute visualisation for the fear of running out. One central image (a stone reservoir with water leaving and water arriving at the same pace) held in mind for the length of the practice. The image replaces the empty-bucket image the worried mind tends to default to.
New to visualisation? Read
how visualisations work
first.
The practice
Setting up
Sit somewhere quiet, feet flat on the floor. Close your eyes or let them rest soft on the floor in front of you. Three slow breaths.
The reservoir
Picture a stone reservoir. Round, deep, walled with old grey stone. Standing at the edge, looking down into the water. The water reaches almost to the top of the wall. Steady, dark, holding.
Notice the temperature of the stone under your hand on the wall. Cool, slightly damp. The smell of water. Light catching the surface of the reservoir, slightly moving.

On one side of the reservoir, a small outlet. Water flows out steadily — clear, unhurried. This is the money leaving: bills paid, food bought, the day's transactions. The water leaves at a steady pace.

On the other side, a small inflow. Water arrives — also clear, also steady. The same pace. This is the money coming in: the work, the unexpected return, the things you haven't seen yet. The water arrives at the same pace as it leaves.

The level of the reservoir stays the same. The water is moving (leaving on one side, arriving on the other) but the reservoir holds steady. Stand at the wall and watch this for one slow minute. Notice the level not dropping.
Notice that the reservoir has been here for longer than you have been at the wall. It will be here after you leave. Its job is to hold; its design is to refill.
Coming back
Let the image fade. Stay with the eyes closed for thirty seconds longer than feels necessary. Then open the eyes slowly. Notice the room around you — the chair, the light, the breath.
If a detail stayed with you (a colour, a sound, a feeling about the level) write one line about it. Often the surprising detail is the part of the practice that lands.
When this isn't working
If the image refuses to settle, if the worry is loud enough to drown the picture, tap first.
Tapping for daily money panic
covers the running-out fear, and the reservoir image often lands more easily after a tapping round.
Where this practice comes from

Reservoir / water-as-money imagery is a public-domain visualisation lineage with deep roots in folk-prosperity traditions — the idea of money as a flow rather than a fixed stock predates modern money-mindset work by centuries. The specific five-minute single-image form used here is adapted from Day 2 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), which pairs the reservoir visualisation with a tapping practice on the same theme.
